1: A Prayer of David — "Turn your ear toward me, O LORD, and answer me, for I am poor and needy." Hebrews 5:7 describes Christ's prayer life in almost precisely these terms: "During the days of Jesus's life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent crying and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission." The posture of poverty and need before God — the kenosis of the one who "did not consider equality with God something to be grasped" (Philippians 2:6) — is modeled by Christ throughout his earthly life and explicitly in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:36-46).
2: "Preserve my soul, for I am devoted to you; save your servant who trusts in you." The servant-language (avdekha, your servant) applied by David to himself points forward to the Servant of Isaiah 40-55, which the NT applies to Christ. Isaiah 42:1 ("here is my servant, whom I uphold") is cited in Matthew 12:18 as fulfilled in Jesus. The trust of the servant who depends entirely on YHWH for soul-preservation is the faith that Hebrews 12:2 attributes to Jesus: "for the joy set before him he endured the cross, scorning its shame," trusting in the vindication that followed.
3: "Be gracious to me, O Lord, for I cry out to you all day long." The petition for grace and the persistence of the cry — "all day long" — are the marks of continuous prayer. Luke 18:1 ("always pray and not give up") and 1 Thessalonians 5:17 ("pray continually") are grounded in Christ's own practice of sustained prayer. Luke records that Jesus often withdrew to pray (Luke 5:16; Psalms 6:12) and that on the night before the crucifixion he "prayed more earnestly" until his sweat fell like blood (Luke 22:44). The all-day cry is the prayer posture Christ both modeled and commands.
4: "Gladden the soul of your servant, for to you, O Lord, I lift up my soul." The upward lifting of the soul to YHWH as the source of joy anticipates John 17:13 (Christ's prayer: "I say these things while I am still in the world, so that they may have the full measure of my joy within them") and Hebrews 12:2 ("for the joy set before him he endured the cross"). The servant's soul gladdened by God alone is the pattern of Christ who "rejoiced in the Holy Spirit" (Luke 10:21) precisely in the context of the disciples' mission.
5: "For you, O Lord, are good and forgiving, abounding in steadfast love for all who call on you." A direct echo of the Exodus 34:6-7 divine name-formula ("compassionate and gracious... abounding in steadfast love"). In Christ, this name takes flesh: John 1:14 ("full of grace and truth") is John's equivalent of the Exodus formula applied to the incarnate Word. Luke 23:34 ("Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing") is the enacted form of the "forgiving" character of v5 — Christ on the cross embodies the forgiveness and hesed that the divine name proclaims.
6: "Give ear to my prayer, O LORD; listen to the voice of my plea for mercy." The cry for a listening ear is the foundational posture of dependence that Christ models. Hebrews 5:7 ("he was heard because of his reverent submission") confirms that the Father's ear was open to the Son's cries. Psalms 22:24 makes the same claim retrospectively: "he did not despise or scorn the suffering of the afflicted one; he did not hide his face from him but listened to his cry for help." The hearing of Christ's prayers is the guarantee that Christ-united believers will also be heard (1 John 5:14-15).
7: "On the day of my trouble I call to you, for you answer me." The confidence of being heard — "you answer me" — is the trust-claim that Jesus embodies. John 11:41-42 ("Father, I thank you that you have heard me; I knew that you always hear me") shows Christ's certainty of the Father's responsiveness as the basis of his prayer. The resurrection is the ultimate answer to the "day of trouble" of the crucifixion — the Father's response to Christ's prayer arrives not as escape from death but as victory over it.
8: "There is no one like you among the gods, O Lord, and no works compare to yours." The incomparability of YHWH — the mi-kamokha tradition of Exodus 15:11 — is the theological ground on which Christ's unique identity is established. Philippians 2:9-11 ("God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name") gives the name above all names to Jesus. 1 Corinthians 8:6 identifies Christ as the Lord through whom all things exist. The incomparable God reveals himself to be Father, Son, and Spirit — the "no one like you" applies to the triune God revealed in Christ.
9: "All the nations you have made will come and bow before you, O Lord, and they will glorify your name." A direct citation-pattern: Philippians 2:10-11 ("every knee shall bow... and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord") combines this verse with Isaiah 45:23. The nations who will bow before YHWH and glorify his name are, in the NT's reading, those who bow before the risen Christ — because the risen Christ is the form in which YHWH's name and honor are made known among all nations. Revelation 15:4 ("All nations will come and worship before you") is the eschatological fulfillment.
10: "For you are great and you do wonders; you alone are God." The exclusivity — "you alone are God" — is the monotheistic affirmation that the NT reframes in a Trinitarian direction without compromising. John 10:30 ("I and the Father are one") and John 1:1 ("the Word was God") place Christ within the divine identity. The "wonders" that YHWH alone does are what Acts 2:22 attributes to Jesus: "a man accredited by God to you by miracles, wonders and signs." The "God alone" who does wonders does them through the Son.
11: "Teach me your way, O LORD, that I may walk in your truth; give me an undivided heart to reverence your name." The "undivided heart" is the singleness of devotion that characterizes Christ's relationship to the Father. John 8:29 ("I always do what pleases him") and John 17:4 ("I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do") describe an undivided, whole-heart consecration. Hebrews 7:26 ("such a high priest truly meets our need — one who is holy, blameless, pure") is the doctrinal form. The Spirit works this same undivided heart in believers (Ezekiel 36:26; Hebrews 10:22).
12: "I will thank you, O Lord my God, with my whole heart; I will glorify your name forever." The whole-heart thanksgiving and eternal glorification of the divine name are the liturgical posture of the Son who glorifies the Father. John 17:1 ("Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you") — the glorification is mutual and eternal. Hebrews 13:15 ("continually offer to God a sacrifice of praise — the fruit of lips that openly profess his name") gives the community form of whole-heart thanksgiving, enabled by Christ as the new covenant high priest.
13: "For your steadfast love toward me is great; you have rescued my soul from the lowest depths of Sheol." A direct anticipation of the resurrection. "Rescued from the lowest depths of Sheol" is the language Acts 2:24 applies to the resurrection: "God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him." Hebrews 13:20 ("the God of peace, who through the blood of the eternal covenant brought back from the dead our Lord Jesus") is the doxological form of this rescue. The great hesed that motivates the rescue is the covenant love that sends the Son into death and raises him from it.
14: "O God, arrogant men have risen up against me; a band of ruthless men seeks my life — they do not keep you before them." The portrait of enemies who seek the life of the anointed one without keeping God before them is the pattern fulfilled in the passion narrative. Mark 14:1 ("the chief priests and teachers of the law were looking for some way to arrest Jesus secretly and kill him") and John 11:53 ("from that day on they plotted to take his life") describe the ruthless men seeking the life of the one God anointed. "They do not keep you before them" is the practical atheism of those who treat Jesus as a problem to be solved rather than the Son of God.
15: "But you, O Lord, are a compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness." The Exodus 34:6-7 formula again — the pivot from enemy-threat (v14) to divine character (v15) is the pattern of confident faith. John 1:14 ("full of grace and truth") translates this formula into the incarnate Word. James 5:11 ("the Lord is full of compassion and mercy") cites this character of God as the ground of perseverance under suffering. Christ's compassion for the crowd (Matthew 9:36), his slowness to anger toward the disciples, and his abundant steadfast love enacted in the cross all embody this verse's portrait of divine character.
16: "Turn to me and be gracious to me; give your strength to your servant, and save the son of your maidservant." The phrase "son of your maidservant" — a servant born in the household, with the deepest covenant ties to the master — describes the intimacy of the servant relationship. Isaiah 49:5 uses similar servant-birth language of the Servant who is formed from the womb to bring Jacob back to God. The giving of divine strength to the servant is what Luke 4:14 describes: Jesus returned from the wilderness "in the power of the Spirit." The prayer for strength is answered in the Spirit's empowering presence.
17: "Show me a sign of your favor, so that those who hate me will see and be ashamed, because you, O LORD, have helped me and comforted me." The sign of divine favor that vindicates the righteous against those who hate them is the resurrection. Matthew 12:39-40 ("no sign will be given... except the sign of the prophet Jonah") — the resurrection is the only sign given. Acts 2:32-36 presents the resurrection as the vindication proving that those who rejected Christ crucified "both Lord and Messiah." The shame of the haters is not gloated over but is the inverse of the vindication: the resurrection proves the Father's help and comfort, defeating the enmity's claims.
